So now you have the open, okay, which is your introduction. You have the main body of your presentation. Now lastly, let's get to the close. And this is the section where you actually ask for the order. Now I'll tell you something very ironic here. When I train sales forces around the world, and I do this all over the world, one of the things that I very often find when I'm called to train a sales force that's not performing well, is that the sales people typically don't ask for the order very much, if at all. They dance around the idea. They don't say, you know, give me a shot, you know, pick up this amount of product. They dance around the order and just leave it sort of open-ended. So that leads me to this question. How many times should you actually ask for the order? Well, the answer is certainly not once. And it's also not twice. It's typically, believe it or not, four, five, or even six or seven times that you directly have to ask the client for the order before they say yes. Okay. And just so you know, this is not about pressuring them. Because if you do this in the wrong way, they will feel pressured. This is about tonality. You're not going to say, give me one shot, Bill. You will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? I mean, people will run away from you. It's going to be like, John, you give me one shot and believe me, you will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? Now that's the tonal pattern that will result in a close. Okay. It's not, you know, give me one shot. Give me one. That's what high pressure sales will do. This is about getting someone to a place where they're really, really certain about your product. They're really, really certain about you as the influencer. They're really, really certain that your company is a solid stand-up company. Then you lower their action threshold and on a bit of pain if necessary, then you say, give me one shot and believe me. You're going to be very, very impressed. Sound fair enough? Absolute reasonableness. That's a language pattern with the right tone that closes sales. Give me one shot and believe me. You will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? Or believe me. You give me one shot and the only problem you'll have as an employee six months ago and get you started them. Sound fair enough? That tonality you can ask for the order five, six, or even seven times. But I promise you this, if you say, you give me one shot, Bill, and believe me, you're not going to be sorry. They'll hang up by the second one. So again, it's apples and oranges. Again, this is the artistry. It's about taking the exact right words, these powerful words that I'm giving you right now. It's a powerful closing pattern and combining it with the exact right tonality. And together, when you have words and tonality and when in person, the body language, those three forms of communication melded together in the right syntax, the right order, you do indeed get to that point in the straight line where you can and will close everyone who's closed-able.
